label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is about what advice Wesla Whitfield would most likely give to other disabled people based on the article. First, I need to look at what the article says about her. She was paralyzed from the waist down after a shooting when she was 29. She went through depression and even tried to kill herself. But then she recovered and learned to adjust. She mentions that the only thing she lost was the ability to walk, but she still had her mind, which is the most valuable asset. She also says that the only real disability is losing your mind. She also mentions that when she feels frustrated (like when everyone is dancing), she removes herself to focus on what she can do. So, the key points are her realization that losing the ability to walk is not the real disability; losing one's mind is. She advises focusing on what you can do rather than what you've lost. She also removed herself from situations that made her frustrated to focus on her abilities. Looking at the options: A) Ignore what you have lost and make the best use of what you have. B) Be modest and hard-working to earn respect from other people. C) Acquire a skill so that you can still be successful and famous. D) Try to sing whenever you feel upset and depressed. Option A seems aligned with her advice to focus on what you can do. She emphasizes using her mind (which implies using what you have left) and not letting the physical disability define her. She "ignored" the loss of walking in a way by not letting it stop her. Option B talks about modesty and hard work. While she is described as witty and humble, the article doesn't specifically mention these as the advice she would give. The focus is more on mindset than on working hard to earn respect. Option C is about acquiring a skill for success. But she didn't acquire a new skill necessarily; she continued with her singing career, which she already had. The focus was on mindset, not acquiring new skills. Option D suggests singing when upset. While she is a singer, she didn't specifically mention singing as a solution to depression. She went through treatment and focused on her mind, but there's no direct advice to sing in those moments. So the best answer is A. She advises focusing on what you can do (make the best use of what you have) and not
